claims.claim_text: `/proc` is a pseudo-filesystem that exposes live kernel process data structures and metadata.
claims.rationale: The upstream proc(5) text directly defines proc as a pseudo-filesystem that provides an interface to kernel data structures.

claims.claim_text: `/proc/$PID/cmdline` exposes command-line arguments, while `/proc/$PID/exe` is a symlink to the executed binary and shows `(deleted)` if that pathname has been unlinked.
claims.rationale: The upstream proc(5) page directly documents both `/proc/[pid]/cmdline` and `/proc/[pid]/exe`, including the appended `(deleted)` marker for an unlinked executable path.

claims.claim_text: `/proc/$PID/environ` shows the initial environment passed at exec time, with entries separated by NUL bytes.
claims.rationale: The upstream proc(5) page explicitly says `/proc/[pid]/environ` contains the initial environment set at exec and that entries are separated by null bytes.

claims.claim_text: `/proc/$PID/limits` shows soft and hard resource limits, and `/proc/$PID/fd/` contains one symlink per open file descriptor.
claims.rationale: The upstream proc(5) page directly documents `/proc/[pid]/limits` and `/proc/[pid]/fd/` with these semantics.

claims.claim_text: Specific wait-channel names such as `do_epoll_wait` or `futex_wait_queue` reliably indicate what kind of thing a process is waiting on.
claims.anchor_text: Use the wait channel to choose a filter. `do_epoll_wait` usually points toward socket or event-loop waiting, `futex_wait_queue` toward locks or runtime scheduling, and filesystem wait channels toward storage or mount health.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Use the wait channel to choose a filter. Wait-channel names can hint at event-loop waiting, locking, or storage issues, but the exact names vary by kernel and workload.
claims.rationale: The general lesson that `/proc/[pid]/wchan` exposes a symbolic sleep location is well supported, but the exact symbolic names are kernel-version-specific examples rather than stable universal facts.

claims.claim_text: `strace` intercepts system calls and received signals, and by default writes trace output to standard error unless `-o` is used.
claims.rationale: The upstream strace manual source states that strace records system calls and signals and prints them to standard error or a file set with `-o`.

claims.claim_text: `strace` supports syscall-set filters for file, process, and network activity; `-tt` adds microsecond timestamps, `-T` adds per-syscall duration, and `-f` follows child processes created by fork, vfork, or clone.
claims.rationale: The upstream strace manual source documents `-f`, the filter sets for file/process/network syscalls, and the `-tt` and `-T` timing options.

claims.claim_text: In Linux process-state reporting, `R` means runnable, `S` interruptible sleep, `D` uninterruptible sleep, `Z` zombie, and load average counts tasks in `R` or waiting in `D`.
claims.rationale: The upstream proc(5) page directly defines these state codes and also states that load average counts runnable jobs and tasks waiting for disk I/O.

claims.claim_text: An uninterruptibly sleeping Linux process may not terminate immediately even if sent `SIGKILL`.
claims.rationale: The cited page explicitly notes that an uninterruptibly sleeping process may not terminate even when sent SIGKILL.

claims.claim_text: A zombie process has already exited and remains in the process table until a parent collects its exit status with a wait-family call.
claims.rationale: The cited page directly explains that a zombie is an exited child whose entry remains until the parent reads exit status via wait.

claims.claim_text: An unlinked file can remain invisible in the directory tree while still consuming disk space until every process holding it open finishes using it.
claims.span_hint: line 208 | section: Core Section 2; lines 612-618 | section: Did You Know?
claims.rationale: The cited page directly explains that deletion of an unlinked file is deferred until processes with access to it have finished using it.

claims.claim_text: `lsof` reports open files and handles, including sockets, pipes, directories, devices, and deleted files, which is why it is useful for disk-full, port-conflict, and unmount investigations.
claims.rationale: This is a concrete tool-capability claim best backed by the lsof manual, but that host is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: `ltrace` traces dynamic library calls for executed processes and is mainly a library-call view rather than a syscall view.
claims.rationale: The official ltrace project page directly describes ltrace as recording dynamic library calls, but the domain is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Linux exposes per-thread information under `/proc/$PID/task`, with one subdirectory per thread and potentially different per-thread status details.
claims.rationale: The upstream proc(5) page directly documents `/proc/[pid]/task` as one subdirectory per thread with per-thread files that may differ from the process-wide view.

claims.claim_text: A futex is a fast user-space locking primitive; the kernel futex syscall is used when threads need to block or wake on a shared condition.
claims.rationale: The upstream futex(2) page directly defines futexes as fast user-space locking and explains that the kernel participates when a thread blocks or wakes.

claims.claim_text: `nsenter` runs a program in selected namespaces of a target process, and a network namespace has its own routing tables, firewall rules, sockets, and `/proc/net` view.
claims.rationale: The upstream nsenter manual source explicitly describes running a program in selected namespaces and the independent networking view provided by a network namespace.

claims.claim_text: In Kubernetes, `kubectl debug` can add an ephemeral container to a running Pod; `--target` targets another container's process namespace, runtime support affects whether other processes are visible, and `--share-processes` allows containers in a copied Pod to see each other's processes.
claims.span_hint: lines 563-573 | section: Playbook: Kubernetes 1.35+ Workload Debugging
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes debug-running-pod documentation directly covers ephemeral containers, `--target`, runtime-dependent process visibility, and `--share-processes` behavior.

further_reading.title: proc(5)
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary low-level reference for `/proc`, per-thread task directories, `wchan`, `limits`, and file-descriptor views.

further_reading.title: strace(1) upstream manual source
further_reading.why_relevant: Best source for trace filters, attach behavior, child following, and timing flags.

further_reading.title: nsenter(1) upstream manual source
further_reading.why_relevant: Clarifies exactly which namespaces you can enter and what changes when you debug from that process context.

further_reading.title: Debug Running Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the official Kubernetes workflow for ephemeral containers, copied debug pods, and process-namespace visibility.

notes: No existing section pool file was found for `linux/operations/troubleshooting`, so `section_pool_ref` is null. Quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, Mermaid, code blocks, and tables were excluded unless adjacent prose made a standalone technical claim. GitHub raw mirrors of upstream man-page sources were preferred where possible so the seed stays on-allowlist; `lsof` and `ltrace` still require allowlist expansion for their primary manuals.
